questions.question: Explain the differences between Phishing, Spear Phishing, and Whaling attacks.
keyPoints: Phishing is mass-scale attack targeting many users with generic messages
Spear Phishing targets specific individuals/organizations with personalized information
Whaling targets high-level executives (C-level) with high-value financial goals
Success rate increases from phishing to whaling due to targeting specificity

questions.question: Describe the three mechanisms of email authentication: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC.
keyPoints: SPF (Sender Policy Framework) - DNS TXT record specifying authorized mail servers
DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail) - Cryptographic signature verification
DMARC - Combines SPF and DKIM with policy enforcement and reporting
All three work together to prevent email spoofing

questions.question: What is Business Email Compromise (BEC)? How can organizations protect against it?
keyPoints: BEC involves attackers impersonating executives/vendors to trick employees
Primary goal is transferring money or stealing sensitive data
Protection includes: verification procedures, multi-factor authentication, employee training
Financial transfer policies requiring multiple approvals

questions.question: Explain S/MIME and PGP email encryption methods with their differences.
keyPoints: S/MIME uses X.509 certificates from Certificate Authorities (hierarchical trust)
PGP uses web of trust model (users certify each other's keys)
S/MIME is more enterprise-friendly, PGP offers more user control
Both provide encryption and digital signatures for email confidentiality and integrity

questions.question: List and explain five common email threats in modern organizations.
keyPoints: Phishing - disguised emails to steal information
Email Spoofing - forged sender addresses
Malware Distribution - malicious attachments/links
Business Email Compromise - financial fraud
Man-in-the-Middle - interception attacks

questions.question: What is email header analysis and why is it important for security?
keyPoints: Header analysis examines email metadata for authentication results
Shows SPF, DKIM, DMARC pass/fail status
Reveals originating IP address and routing path
Helps identify spoofed emails and tracing attack sources

questions.question: Explain the concept of email sandboxing and its role in comprehensive email security.
keyPoints: Sandboxing executes attachments in isolated virtual environment
Detects malicious behavior without risking production systems
Identifies zero-day threats and advanced malware
Part of defense-in-depth strategy alongside other security measures

questions.question: Discuss comprehensive email security best practices that organizations should implement.
keyPoints: Implement email authentication: SPF, DKIM, DMARC
Use encryption: TLS for transit, S/MIME/PGP for end-to-end
Deploy email filtering and sandboxing solutions
Regular employee security awareness training
Implement strong password policies and multi-factor authentication
Establish clear procedures for handling suspicious emails
Regular security audits and incident response planning
Keep email server software updated with latest patches

questions.question: Explain how SPF, DKIM, and DMARC work together to provide comprehensive email authentication. Include a diagram in your answer.
keyPoints: SPF validates the sending server is authorized for the domain (DNS lookup)
DKIM validates message integrity using cryptographic signatures
DMARC provides unified policy framework combining both
DMARC policy modes: none (monitor), quarantine (suspicious), reject (block)
Reporting mechanism allows domain owners to monitor authentication
All three form defense-in-depth: SPF checks envelope sender, DKIM checks headers, DMARC enforces policy

questions.question: A company has experienced several phishing attacks resulting in credential theft. Design a comprehensive email security strategy to prevent such attacks.
keyPoints: Implement multi-layered email security approach
Technical controls: SPF/DKIM/DMARC authentication, email filtering, sandboxing
Encryption: STARTTLS for transit, S/MIME for sensitive communications
User education: regular phishing awareness training and simulations
Procedures: verify unusual requests via separate channel
Technical controls: advanced threat protection, URL rewriting, attachment scanning
Incident response plan for handling successful phishing attacks
Regular security assessments and updates

questions.question: Explain the concept of Man-in-the-Middle attacks on email and discuss mitigation strategies.
keyPoints: MITM attacks intercept email communications during transit
Attackers can eavesdrop, modify content, or redirect messages
Common in unsecured networks or compromised mail servers
TLS/STARTTLS encryption prevents interception
End-to-end encryption (PGP/S/MIME) provides stronger protection
Certificate verification prevents rogue server attacks
Regular security audits of email infrastructure
